Antibodies and Diagnostic Testing
Barbara Spinda, MS, MLS(ASCP)CM SMCM
Clinical Assistant Professor | Program Director
Division of Clinical Laboratory Science
Vera Z. Dwyer College of Health Sciences
bspinda@iu.edu

Objectives
01. Vaccines and immunity
Briefly review how vaccines elicit an immune response.
02. Vaccines
Compare and contrast different types of vaccines.
03. Testing for immunity
What does this even mean?
04. Considering vaccines and disease
Evaluate the impact of Hepatitis B vaccine on diagnosing disease.
05. The future of diagnostics and vaccines
Explore what the future might bring.
Hematopoietic Cells
Immunity

T lymphocytes
Cytotoxic T cells (CD8)​
Helper T cells (CD4)
Recognize and remove virus infected cells and cancer cells​
Have granules containing cytotoxins that cause apoptosis = cell death
Produce cytokines (signal proteins)​
Activate other immune cells against bacteria and parasites​
Alert B cells to make antibodies ​
B lymphocytes

First encounter​
Lag phase – may take a few days to a week​
When cells begin to know there is something foreign around​
Antigen presenting cells show antigen to T cells  activate B cells ​
Log phase – lots of B cells made​
Antibodies begin made – IgM first, then IgG​
Some B cells become memory cells​
Second encounter​
Anamnestic response – B cells remember!​
IgM is made quickly ​
Lots of IgG made faster than first time​
Battle won much quicker than first time​
Antigens and Antibodies
Vaccines

What is a vaccine?
A biological product that can be used to safely induce an immune response that confers protection against infection and/or disease on subsequent exposure to a pathogen (Pollard & Bijker, 2021, p. 83).​
​Does not cause disease ​
Artificially mimics infection ​
Generates primary immune response = creation of memory B cells
Vaccine Categories

Types of Vaccines
Live active
No modification
Small amount = mild disease
No longer used
Example: smallpox
Live attenuated
Modified – weaken or inactivate
Pathogenic part removed
Widely used
Examples: measles mumps, rubella, chickenpox, influenza, oral polio….
Whole killed
Whole cell used – killed or nonviable
Example: whole-cell pertussis, influenza, polio, hepatitis A, rabies, 1896 - typhoid
Toxoid
Modification of toxin from bacteria
Anti-toxoid antibody produced
Short lived – 10 years
Example: tetanus, 1923 - diphtheria

Types of Vaccines
Virus-like particles
Looks like the virus…. But isn’t
Example: human papillomavirus
Subunit
Protein, polysaccharide, or peptide
Small amount – just enough for immune response
Example: pertussis, hepatitis A & B, meningococcus, pneumococcus, 1970 - anthrax
Outer membrane vesicle
Gram negative bacteria
Piece of cell wall membrane with antigen
Example: 1987 – Haemophilus influenzae type B
Protein-polysaccharide conjugate
Polysaccharide bound to carrier protein
Example: Haemophilus influenzae type B, pneumococcus, typhoid, 1987 - meningogoccus
The Infamous mRNA Vaccine
Testing for Immunity

What does it mean to have immunity?
“a condition of being able to resist a particular disease especially through preventing development of a pathogenic microorganism or by counteracting the effects of its products”
-Merriam-Webster Dictionary
Testing for immunity is common practice for healthcare providers…. Why?
What should we look for to know if someone is immune?

Immunoassays
Antigen-detecting immunoassays
Antibody-detecting immunoassays
Y
Y
Y
Y
Y

Testing Health Professionals
Measles
Mumps
Rubella
Varicella

Vidas 3
Solid phase receptacle (SPR)
Has antigen bound to inside
All reactions happen in SPR
Reagent strip
Specimen: serum or plasma (lithium heparin or EDTA
Procedure:
Specimen is diluted (100µL into 500µL diluent)
Specimen sucked into SPR – incubated
Antibodies in specimen bind to antigen bound to SPR
Residual elements washed
Phosphatase-labeled mouse monoclonal anti-human IgG antibodies sucked into SPR – incubated
Antibodies bind to antigen-antibody matrix
Residual elements washed
Conjugate (4-Methyl-umbelliferyl phosphate) cycled through SPR
Catalyzes the hydrolysis of substrate into fluorescent product
Fluorescence read at 450nm
Y
Y

Results
Positive =
Negative =
Equivocal =
You’re immune!
You need a booster
Test again in 2-3 weeks… or get a booster
Vaccines and Disease

Hepatitis B virus (HBV)
Family: Hepadinoviridae
Long recognized as significant cause of liver damage associated with morbidity and mortality
Pleomorphic, partially dsDNA, icosahedral capsule with envelop
Transmissions: humans are reservoir and vector; direct contact – body secretions, recipient of contaminated blood products, percutaneous injection of virus, perinatal exposure
Incubation: 1-3 months
Disease: acute infection with resolution (90%); fulminant hepatitis (1%); chronic hepatitis (9%)
400 million cases worldwide
Diagnosis: serology, viral antigen detection, and PCR
Treatment: antivirals and liver transplant
Prevention: HBV vaccine; hepatitis B immunoglobulins
Vaccine - 3 part series; 2nd dose at least 4 weeks after 1st, 3rd dose at least 8 weeks after 2nd and 16 weeks after 1st

Hepatitis B antigens and antibodies
HBsAg – surface antigen
Most reliable marker
Found weeks before evidence of liver damage
IgM anti-HBc – core antigen
Appears early in the course of disease
Anti-HBs – 6 months after acute infection
Indicates recovery and immunity
Best indication of active viral replication – HBV DNA
Quantitation predicts patient response to treatment
Interpreting Hepatitis B results
The Future of Diagnostics and Vaccines

We are talking about syphilis
Syphilis – Treponema pallidum
Cannot culture
Serology testing used most often
Dark field microscopy or fluorescent antibody - $
PCR tests - $$

What is a rapid plasma reagin (RPR) test?
Identifies antibodies against substances released by infected cells
Cardiolipin (lipid) and lecithin (mixture of glycerophospholipids)
NOT treponeme-specific antibodies
Patient plasma tested

What is a rapid plasma reagin (RPR) test?
Bioplex 2200

Back to the study…
Patient samples
38 patients
Time 0 = up to 1 week before vaccine
Time 1 and 2 = 2 weeks after each vaccine dose
Time 3-7 = monthly after second vaccine dose
Tests performed
Using Bio-Rad instrument (EIA)
ß2GP1 IgA, IgG, IgM
Cardiolipin IgA, IgG, IgM
EBV IgG (3), IgM (2)
HSV-1, HSV-2 IgG
Lyme IgG
Rubella IgG, IgM
Mumps IgG
Measles IgG
Varicella zoster IgG
Toxoplasma IgG, IgM
CMV IgG, IgM
HIV ag, HIV-1 IgG, HIV-2 IgG
Syphilis ab
RPR
RPR – Sure-Vue, Macro-Vue
Interpretations
NR  NR
NR  R
R  R
R  NR
Negative before vaccine… still negative!
Negative before vaccine… now positive
Positive before vaccine… still positive
Positive before vaccine… now negative
Results

Questions…
Why might we see these results with RPR testing?
What limitations do you see to this study?
Vaccine uses lipid nanoparticles to transport mRNA
Ionizable lipid, phospholipid, cholesterol, pegylated lipid
Antibodies may be created against LNPs
Only tested 38 patients – we need more!
Need to test different populations - all patients were anti-treponeme negative
High risk HIV positive patients are often treponemal positive – will their results be affected?
People with RPR reactivity due to nonsyphilis conditions vs previously-infected individual
Why does the Bioplex show more false positives?
Bioplex uses fluorescent detection
Manual tests use technologist eyes
Why is this important for laboratory professionals to know?
